feed.title: Markus Löning
feed.subtitle: My blog on machine learning and software development

entry.title: PyPI downloads analysis
entry.content:
More details

  • To set up Google BigQuery, take a look here: https://packaging.python.org/guides/analyzing-pypi-package-downloads/#analyzing-pypi-package-downloads
  • I use pandas_gbq to run queries: https://pandas-gbq.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html
 

import os
import pandas_gbq as gbq
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from datetime import datetime

PROJECT_ID = "" # add Google BigQuery project-id

# projects to analyse
projects = (
    "sktime",
    "tsfresh",
    "tslearn",
    "gluonts",
    "statsmodels",
    "u8darts",
    "hcrystalball",
    "Featuretools",
    "adtk",
    "fbprophet",
    "pysf",
    "pyflux",
    "pmdarima",
    "ruptures",
    "seqlearn",
    "seglearn",
    "stumpy",
    "tseries",
    "cesium",
    "sktime-dl",
    "nitime"
)

# time frame to look back
n_months = 24

Download data 

today = datetime.today()
file = f"pypi-download-data-{today.year}-{today.month}.csv"

# load from disk when downloaded data exists for current month
if os.path.isfile(file):
    print("Loading file from disk ... ")
    results = pd.read_csv(file, index_col=0)

# otherwise download fresh data
else:
    print("Downloading data from Google Big Query ... ")
    # standard SQL code
    sql = f"""
    SELECT
      COUNT(*) AS n_downloads,
      file.project as project,
      SUBSTR(_TABLE_SUFFIX, 1, 6) AS month
    FROM `the-psf.pypi.downloads*`
    WHERE
      file.project IN {projects}
      AND _TABLE_SUFFIX
        BETWEEN FORMAT_DATE(
          '%Y%m01', DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL {n_months} MONTH))
        AND FORMAT_DATE('%Y%m%d', CURRENT_DATE())
    GROUP BY project, month
    ORDER BY project, month DESC
    """
    results = gbq.read_gbq(sql, project_id=PROJECT_ID)

    results.month = pd.to_datetime(results.month, format='%Y%m').dt.to_period(freq='m')
    results.to_csv(file)

results.head()

Downloading data from Google Big Query ... 

Downloading: 100%|██████████| 436/436 [00:00<00:00, 443.20rows/s]
n_downloads project month
0 3692 adtk 2020-12
1 42761 adtk 2020-11
2 37532 adtk 2020-10
3 24671 adtk 2020-09
4 14009 adtk 2020-08

Plot monthly downloads 

items = [
    "sktime",
    "tsfresh",
    "tslearn",
    "gluonts",
    "statsmodels",
    "u8darts",
    "hcrystalball",
    "fbprophet",
    "pmdarima",
    "stumpy",
]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, figsize=plt.figaspect(.4))
(results
 .pivot(index="month", columns="project", values="n_downloads")
 .filter(items=items)
 .plot(ax=ax)
)
ax.set(yscale="log", xlabel="Time period (months)", ylabel="Number of monthly downloads", title=f"Number of monthyl PyPI downloads");
plt.legend(title="Packages", loc='upper left')
# plt.savefig("pypi-downloads.png", dpi=300);

<matplotlib.legend.Legend at 0x7fd1bcb54690>
  • See Matthew Rocklin's blog post on estimating package users
  • Many of these downloads likely come from bots (continuous intgration, etc)
  • Note that sktime depends on some of these packages (e.g. pmdarima, statsmodels), so for every time someone downloads sktime, they also download these packages.

Plot total downloads 

totals = results.groupby("project").sum()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, figsize=plt.figaspect(.25))
totals.sort_values("n_downloads", ascending=True).plot.barh(ax=ax, legend=False)
ax.set(xscale="log", xlabel="Number of PyPI downloads", title=f"Number of PyPI downloads over the last {n_months} months");

entry.title: Dynamic init creation
entry.content:
Preliminaries 

!pip install sktime fbprophet

Motivation

The main goal of sktime is to develop a unified framework for machine learning with time series. We do this by creating a common interface for different types of algorithms. Instead of re-implementing algorithms from scratch, we try to interface existing algorithm implementations whenever possible and merely expose them through a common interface. This often requires writing the constructor (__init__.py) with the appropriate arguments from the algorithm that we want to interface.

In this blog post, I discuss how we can create the constructor automatically based on the constructor of the interfaced algorithm and any additional arguments we may want to add.

I saw the idea originally in the HCrystalball package, but cleaned up the functions a little bit to make them more readable.

The idea relies on dynamic function creation, you can find a good discussion of that topic in this blog post.

Writing a decorator for dynamic init creation 

import inspect
from types import FunctionType
from fbprophet import Prophet
from sktime.forecasting.base._base import BaseForecaster

def _get_param_dict(signature):
    return {
        p.name: p.default if p.default != inspect.Parameter.empty else None
        for p in signature.parameters.values()
        if p.name != "self" and p.kind != p.VAR_KEYWORD and p.kind != p.VAR_POSITIONAL
    }

def _get_params(func):
    signature = inspect.signature(func)
    return _get_param_dict(signature)

def create_init(model_cls):
    """Decorator to dynamically create init function based on wrapped `model_cls`"""

    def new_init(base_init):
        # combine params from wrapper class and wrapped model
        params = _get_params(model_cls.__init__)
        params.update(_get_params(base_init))

        # compile function code from string representation
        assignments = "; ".join([f"self.{name} = {name}" for name in params.keys()])
        string = f'def __init__(self, {", ".join(params.keys())}): {assignments}'
        code = compile(string, "<string>", "exec")

        return FunctionType(code.co_consts[0], base_init.__globals__, name="__init__", argdefs=tuple(params.values()))

    return new_init

Example

In this example, we use fbprophet.

class ProphetForecaster(BaseForecaster):
    """Test docstring"""

    @create_init(Prophet)
    def __init__(foo=1, bar=2):
        pass

f = ProphetForecaster()
f.get_params()

{'bar': 2,
 'changepoint_prior_scale': 0.05,
 'changepoint_range': 0.8,
 'changepoints': None,
 'daily_seasonality': 'auto',
 'foo': 1,
 'growth': 'linear',
 'holidays': None,
 'holidays_prior_scale': 10.0,
 'interval_width': 0.8,
 'mcmc_samples': 0,
 'n_changepoints': 25,
 'seasonality_mode': 'additive',
 'seasonality_prior_scale': 10.0,
 'stan_backend': None,
 'uncertainty_samples': 1000,
 'weekly_seasonality': 'auto',
 'yearly_seasonality': 'auto'}

Future work

A few further considerations:

  • It would be preferable to use the CodeType replace method, but it's only available from Python >=3.8, see https://bugs.python.org/issue37032
  • Ideally, we would want to add minor extensions to unify parameter names (e.g. sp for seasonality, n_jobs for multiprocessing)?
  • Can we automatically update the docstring too?
